data.title: Microsoft Stock Makes No Sense, Play The Spending Versus Revenue Gap
data.description:
Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) reported its second-quarter fiscal 2026 earnings in January, and based on the numbers, it was a solid beat. Revenue came in at $81.3 billion, topping both the Wall Street consensus by 1.24% and management's own guidance range of $79.5 billion to $80.6 billion.

Meanwhile, diluted EPS (non-GAAP) hit $4.14, topping estimates by 5.69%. Not only that, Azure is all but sold out. The fact that the stock dropped 19% as a result makes no sense. On paper, this was a beat-and-raise quarter, the kind of report that generally gets rewarded.

However, the market did what it did and focused on how fast Microsoft is spending compared with how fast that money is turning into revenue. Classic overreaction given the company's pipeline, which this analysis will cover.

With that in mind, my rating for Microsoft remains a Strong Buy amid the selloff.

What was in Microsoft’s second-quarter fiscal 2026 earnings report?

Under the hood, one of the most notable aspects of Microsoft’s latest earnings report is its cloud business.

For the first time, Microsoft Cloud revenue crossed the $50 billion mark in a single quarter, growing 26% year over year to $51.5 billion. That is a notable milestone. Microsoft is now generating over $200 billion in annualized revenue from cloud services alone.

Within the cloud, Azure and other cloud services increased 39%. That’s a notable growth rate for a business this size, though it’s a step down from the 40% growth rate it posted in the prior first quarter, which likely contributed to Wall Street’s negative reaction despite the overall quarterly beat.

Meanwhile, the broader Intelligent Cloud business brought in $32.9 billion, which is up 29% from the same period last year. This segment includes Azure, along with server products and enterprise services.

Then there’s Productivity and Business Processes, housing Office 365, LinkedIn, and Dynamics 365. This segment’s revenue for the recent quarter increased 16% to $34.1 billion. This business remains Microsoft’s workhorse, as it doesn’t grab the headlines that cloud and AI businesses do, but it still pulls in a good amount of cash with strong margins.

Now, one of the areas closely worth watching is the Copilot rollout. This is Microsoft’s AI assistant, which is being integrated into Office 365 apps like Word and Teams. Early signs suggest it’s helping with enterprise renewal rates, so companies are potentially renewing or upgrading their Microsoft subscriptions, but it is still too early to assume that trend is largely driven by the Copilot rollout.

And then there’s the More Personal Computing segment, which appears to be the current weak point of Microsoft. This segment covers Windows, Xbox, Surface, and search, and the quarter's revenue declined 3% to $14.3 billion, with the Xbox content and services segment falling 5% year over year. This segment has been underperforming for some time.

That said, I think most investors don’t look at this as a dealbreaker, as it’s not where Microsoft is directing the growth dollars. It’s a drag, for sure, but it is not especially concerning.

In terms of profitability, net income for the quarter jumped 60% to $38.5 billion, or $5.18 per share (basic), and while that is impressive, there’s a catch. Microsoft booked $7.6 billion in gains from OpenAI (OPENAI)-related investments during this quarter, while in the same quarter last year, Microsoft actually recorded $939 million in losses from OpenAI.

So, the year-over-year swing from OpenAI items alone was roughly $8.5 billion. When you strip that out, non-GAAP net income growth is 23%, which is the number from the actual operations, and it’s one of the metrics most worth watching. Still, reporting 23% growth for a company bringing in over $305 billion in annual revenue is strong by any measure.

It also tells us that revenue growth is translating into earnings growth at a healthy rate. But there’s also a point of contention to be aware of. Gross margin for the quarter narrowed to 68% from around 69% in the same period last year, driven by the shift toward AI training and inference workloads in the cloud. In other words, running AI is more expensive than running Office 365, and that is showing up in the margins.

That said, this margin compression doesn’t appear permanent, though it could mean another four to six quarters before margins finally level out. It won’t be a quick fix, so investors should size their expectations accordingly.

Why the stock dropped ~19% since earnings

At the time of publication, Microsoft shares trade just under $390 a share, and since the latest quarterly report, the stock has been down roughly 19%. Year to date, it’s also down around 20%. Over the past six months, the stock is down about 25%. And if you zoom out to a 52-week period, Microsoft is flat. For a company of this caliber, that kind of price action is highly unusual.

But what happened? Again, the market focused on spending growing faster than Azure revenue. Capital expenditures jumped over 66% to $37.5 billion year over year, while Azure grew 39% over the same period. So, the gap between spending growth and revenue growth made the market uncomfortable.

At the same time, Microsoft decided to route new GPU capacity toward internal products like Copilot rather than allocating all of it to external Azure customers. That appears to be a strategic decision that makes sense over a three- to five-year window, but it concerned investors who wanted to see spending show up as immediate revenue acceleration.

While it's understandable, the reaction appears overdone, but it still created an opportunity for long-term investors to buy more.

Valuation

At around $390 per share, Microsoft trades at a forward P/E (GAAP) of about 22.61x. This ratio shows how much investors are paying for every dollar of earnings the company is expected to generate over the next 12 months. A lower number generally means you’re paying less for future profit.

In this case, Microsoft’s ~22x multiple is about 21% below the sector median. The largest enterprise company on the planet, the second-largest cloud provider, and arguably the company with the deepest AI integration strategy in the industry is trading at a discount to the average tech stock—that is remarkable.

Here’s a table that lines up Microsoft’s P/E ratio against other Magnificent 7 logos:

As you can see, Microsoft and Nvidia are the only two MAG7 names trading below the sector median P/E. Nvidia sits at 23.71% below the sector median, which makes it potentially undervalued as well. Then look at the rest; everyone trades above the sector median.

So, the idea that Microsoft is expensive doesn’t really hold water, especially when you compare it against the peer group. The market appears to have repriced Microsoft to reflect the spending and other subtle concerns all in one move, but that repricing appears to have overshot. When you strip away the near-term noise around spending and margins, the core business is performing at a very high level. And the current multiple doesn’t reflect that. That gap between price and fundamentals is where the opportunity lives.

What could propel Microsoft stock even higher?

So if the valuation looks attractive, what does Microsoft have in the pipeline that could push the stock higher from here?

The key factor I see is the $625 billion in commercial remaining performance obligations, or RPO. It’s practically the company’s order book, meaning revenue that customers have already committed to, but that has not yet been delivered. It’s essentially the “almost sure” backlog. Microsoft’s RPO increased 110% year over year to $625 billion, and the portion expected to be recognized within the next 12 months increased 39%.

But Microsoft has been upfront regarding the RPO. They confirmed that 45% of that backlog is tied to the expanded partnership with OpenAI, which is a huge chunk. More importantly, the remaining 55% of the pie increased 28% all on its own, which is what’s more important. It means that even if the OpenAI component were completely set aside, Microsoft’s contracted pipeline would still be growing at an impressive pace.

Now, CFO Amy Hood disclosed during the earnings call that the full $625 billion backlog has a weighted average duration of about 2.5 years. Against Microsoft’s current annual revenue base of roughly $305 billion, that works out to around $250 billion per year in revenue recognized from backlog alone. While that doesn’t exceed current revenue, it does cover a significant portion of it and reinforces the strength and visibility of Microsoft’s forward demand.

Moreover, there’s another layer that the market seems to be overlooking. Hood also disclosed that Microsoft holds an $80 billion unfulfilled Azure order backlog due to power constraints. That’s $80 billion in pent-up demand just sitting there, waiting for infrastructure to catch up.

That also ties directly to Azure’s sequential deceleration. According to Hood, Azure growth would have exceeded 40% if Microsoft had routed all the new GPU capacity from the first to the second quarter exclusively to external Azure customers. Instead, Microsoft decided to invest in building out the first-party AI advantages at the cost of making Azure's growth numbers look slower in the near term.

That also appears to make sense over the long run, as it builds a product moat that compounds over time. But it did cost the stock on earnings day, because on the surface, it made Azure growth look like it was cooling down, when in reality, the demand is stronger than ever. It’s the capacity that needs to catch up.

What could continue weighing on Microsoft stock?

Of course, we can’t skip over the risks. Right now, and in the foreseeable future, the CapEx-to-return gap could continue to weigh, no matter how management justifies it. In the recent quarter, Microsoft's spending increased 66% to $37.5 billion compared to the same period last year, and the first half of FY2026 alone totaled $72.4 billion.

Now, the issue isn’t that Microsoft is spending massively. In fact, every major cloud provider is doing the same thing, with Amazon being the highest, with projected 2026 spending of $200 billion. The issue, rather, is the growing gap between how fast that spending is ramping up and how fast the revenue it's meant to generate arrives. Azure, on the other hand, is growing at 39% year over year and decelerating.

On top of that, two-thirds of Microsoft’s spending went to GPUs and CPUs that carry a six-year depreciation schedule (useful life). This is important because once you buy those chips, the depreciation expenses are locked in. Cloud gross margin has already come down from 72% to roughly 65% in about five quarters, and management guided for further declines in the third quarter. Meanwhile, the third-quarter growth guidance came in at 37% to 38%, which means continued deceleration.

What’s my verdict?

Microsoft appears to be facing a timing issue. Revenue is coming in, but it isn’t showing up on the same quarterly cadence as spending.

That’s because building out cloud infrastructure is inherently lumpy. There’s a natural gap between when the money goes out and when the money comes back, and it seems like the market is pricing the spending today but discounting the revenue that follows, and that creates an attractive setup for those with a long-term horizon.

When infrastructure catches up to the contracted demand, and it will because the contracts are already signed, the operating leverage on the other side will be meaningful. With that, my Strong Buy rating for Microsoft remains firmly in place.

data.title: Goldman Sachs: Capital Markets Titan At A Discounted Valuation
data.description:
Goldman Sachs (GS) has been a prime beneficiary of elevated market levels in the past year through surging equity trading revenues and investment banking deals. The stock is down 7.4% YTD versus the banking sector's 4.5% decline, despite outperforming in a one-year period. We believe Goldman Sachs remains a leader in investment banking, and the markets might have cast too much of a worry over its benign exposure to software private credit.

Have global markets peaked?

Investors will have no doubt that the stock trades at a strong correlation with equity and fixed income markets, as its Global Banking & Markets division and Asset & Wealth Management are responsible for 80% and 19% of its total pre-tax income, respectively, in FY2025. With the S&P 500 down ~3% on the year and valuations at a peak, investors have fair concerns about whether GS has topped as well.

While we are wary of the risks rich market premiums may pose, we opine that GS has structural advantages that can still help it thrive in a moderate market downturn.

Investments in equity trading are paying off

According to the Q4 call, the management has attributed its record setting equities net revenues during the quarter to multi-year investments in its prime brokerage and equity financing franchise that have paid off. The shift in institutional behavior towards more active repositioning, driven by AI-related equity rotations and geopolitical uncertainty, has facilitated higher trading volume. Further, the company has also benefited from structural expansion of derivative activities across rates, credit, and equity volatility. We believe that GS benefits more so than its peers when capturing this structural elevation in market activity—in the table, GS has notably higher pre-tax income margins for capital markets/trading segments.

Segment (FY2025) GS MS equiv. Citi equiv. DB equiv.
Capital Markets / IB GBM 42.4% IS 34.0% Markets 27.0% IB ~35%
Wealth / Asset Mgmt AWM 24.7% WM 29.3% Wealth 17.4% Private Bank ~24%​
Asset Management (included in AWM) IM 22.7% Services 33.6% DWS 29.1%​

Source: Companies, Himalayas Research estimates

M&A dominance and banking backlog

GS has reclaimed the top spot in global M&A advisory in 2025, having advised on $1.48T in deal volume and earned $4.6B in fees. Those deals included Alphabet's (GOOGL)(GOOG) $32B acquisition of cloud firm Wiz.

Continuing on FY25's strength (IB fees grew 25% Y/Y to $2.58B in 4Q25), the company foresees a robust FY2026E on AI-related consolidation and financial sponsor pent-up activity. GS has stated that it has a substantial investment banking backlog, and M&A drivers are still intact for the coming year. On the Q4 call, CEO Solomon was optimistic about FY26E as its deal pipeline coming into 2026 is at a four-year high.

While there has been uncertainty around geopolitics and interest rate paths, the volatility around 1Q26 may not dampen deal appetite, as Goldman Sachs's backlog is committed, meaning that the deals will likely happen, yet the timing may shift.

Private credit platform

Goldman Sachs has been building up its private credit platform with $142B in assets, aiming to double to $300B by 2029. The company has made several acquisitions and partnerships in the past few years to solidify its positioning.

The turmoils that have roiled private credit markets of late have spooked investors, bringing down quality franchises like GS. We believe GS is proactive in this space by allowing its hedge fund customers to short baskets of private credit exposure. This is a new structured product to capture volatility both-ways while also warehousing private credit risk.

Also, global co-head of private credit, James Reynolds, mentioned that asset-backed lending and investment grade private credit are the fastest growing sub-segments in 2026, and that mezzanine demand is picking up specifically because the overall M&A market has been robust.

Marquee listings in 2026

There are several key listings potentially hitting in 2026, which include the highly anticipated IPOs for SpaceX, OpenAI, and Anthropic. GS will no doubt be among the banks competing for lead-left roles.

In Europe, GS' EMEA division told reporters that the company has many potential IPO deals with European companies. So, the IPO boom is not just in the U.S. but also around other major equity markets.

CEO David Solomon anticipates ECM levels in FY2026E to exceed that of FY2025, but will remain below the 2021 peak.

How exposed is GS AM to redemptions?

Unlike Ares (ARES), Blue Owl (OWL) and Blackstone (BX), GS is much better positioned and well managed. GS has a material $22B implied software credit book, according to a February 27 letter sent to its AM clients. But the redemption rate is lower than peers, and inflows remain positive.

Metric Goldman Sachs Peer Average
Software/tech credit exposure 15.5% of portfolio (Q3 2025) High end of peer range
Q4 2025 redemption rate 3.5% 5%+ at competitors
December 2025 inflows +11% above YTD average Declining at most peers
Fund structure Primarily closed-end institutional Peers more retail semi-liquid

Sources: Bloomberg, market sources, Himalayas Research estimates

Generally speaking, Goldman Sachs targets companies with $100M+ EBITDA, whose default rates are meaningfully lower than the smaller firms. In terms of fund structure and clientele, Goldman's funds are primarily closed-end and offered to institutional clients, avoiding the redemption spiral risk hitting peers' semi-liquid retail BDCs.

Valuation and verdict

On a forward P/E basis, GS is attractively valued against MS and JPM, despite having similar or arguably superior margins for their markets business. GS is also cheaper valued on P/TBV, mainly due to its still-maturing asset/wealth management business, whose segment margins are behind the leader—Morgan Stanley's 30%.

We believe GS has the most favorable prime brokerage and equity financing franchise, which will likely do well in volatile, moderate downturn environments. Its banking division has a strong pipeline coming into 2026, while its AWM is ramping up and provides market upside exposure, ready to capture a larger share of the market AUM with its growing private credit and alternatives platform.

We rate GS a buy.

data.title: Nomura Core Equity Fund: Q4 2025 Buys, Sells, And Standouts
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Nomura Core Equity Fund Q4 2025 Commentary.

For 4Q25, Nomura Core Equity Fund Institutional Class shares underperformed the Fund's benchmark, the S&P 500 Index. Stock selection drove 90% of the underperformance. Selections were weakest in consumer discretionary, financials, and communication services. Sector allocation decisions were modestly negative with our underweight of healthcare, which outperformed, and overweight of financials, which underperformed, affecting performance. Cash was a small drag on performance given the modest quarterly gain in the market.

Leading contributors on a relative basis to the S&P 500 follow:

Applied Materials Inc. (AMAT) shares increased on positive revisions to 2026 and 2027 semiconductor capital spending plans, a response to surging demand for AI-related chips.

Cummins Inc (CMI). shares experienced steady price gains and positive momentum due to strong revenue and earnings growth, particularly in its power-generation business, which has significant datacenter exposure.

Oracle Corp. (ORCL) shares declined during the quarter. Our lack of ownership was beneficial as investors grew concerned about customer concentration with OpenAI and the large external financing required to fund an aggressive AI infrastructure buildout.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) Co. Ltd. shares continued to appreciate on broad leadership in leading-edge semiconductor manufacturing and positive exposure to key themes within technology including AI buildout.

Advanced Micro Devices Inc (AMD). shares advanced during the quarter on a large customer commitment to the company's next generation AI accelerator chips and indications that customers want competitive options to Nvidia (NVDA) chips.

Leading detractors on a relative basis to the S&P 500 follow:

Fiserv Inc (FI) (FI). shares declined on a material downward revision of 2025 and 2026 growth expectations and the departure of the CFO and three board members. The company admitted to unnaturally boosting prior year growth in the Clover merchant acceptance businesses as well as aggressive growth tactics in the financial technology segment. While the new management team appears to have come clean on suspect accounting and disclosure practices, we exited our position based on low conviction in our prior growth estimates.

AutoZone Inc (AZO). shares declined during the quarter on accelerated investment in future growth via the buildout of new stores and "hubs" that will serve the company's commercial market-share ambitions. These investments are negatively affecting near-term earnings.

Eli Lilly and Co. (LLY) shares surged on a price agreement with the government for its key weight loss drugs. The deal appears to have de-risked near-term growth. Given our long-term concern about rising competition and decelerating growth, we did not own shares in the company and thus did not benefit.

BAE Systems PLC (BAESY) shares declined on speculation that Ukraine and Russia were coming closer to an agreement to end the war. Our thesis in owning BAE is much longer-term in nature and is reliant on a generation shift in defense spending intentions in Europe.

Eaton Corp (ETN). PLC shares declined on disclosure that investments in new manufacturing capacity and acquisitions will limit near-term upward earnings revisions though the company continues to see a robust business environment tied to data-center construction, for which the company provides electrical products.

Positions Added:

Accenture PLC (ACN) is a global technology consulting and managed services business that has thrived during previous periods of technological disruption and change. While early, we believe the need for businesses everywhere to adapt and invest in AI capabilities will enable accelerated growth for Accenture.

Entergy Corp. (ETR) is an electric utility focused on the Southern region of the US including portions of Arkansas, Louisiana, Mississippi, and Texas. We like Entergy's accelerating earnings growth tied to regulated, low-risk capacity investment and contracted backlog of new gas generation capacity to serve large data-center customers. This historic mid-single digit earnings grower should accelerate to the low-double-digits over the coming years.

Ferguson Enterprises Inc. (FERG) is a high-quality distributor of plumbing, infrastructure, HVAC, and appliances. We are optimistic about future growth and valuation upside due to exposure to non-residential investment, including large infrastructure projects, and a bottoming in residential activity. Future inclusion in the S&P500 is an upside technical catalyst.

Gilead Sciences Inc. (GILD) was purchased based on strong expertise in HIV treatment and a revolutionary HIV prevention product, Yeztugo, that we believe will drive meaningful upside earnings revisions.

Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. (TMO) is likely to benefit from improving market conditions for life sciences tools following a three-year post Covid "hangover."

Position Eliminated:

Allstate Corp. (ALL)

ConocoPhillips (COP)

Fiserv Inc.

Linde PLC (LIN)

The Sherwin-Williams Co. (SHW)

Zebra Technologies Corp. (ZBRA)
