data.title: Kanzhun: A 'Buy' On Profitability Surprise And Attractive Yield
data.description:
I've stayed bullish on Kanzhun Limited (BZ) (2076.HK).

Its operating profitability surprised on the upside in the recent quarter. The stock's FY26 capital management (buybacks/dividends) yield is an appealing mid-single digit.

My November 20, 2025 update highlighted that this Chinese "recruitment platform" operator delivered above-expectations 3Q25 results.

I'm Impressed With Quarterly Margin Enhancement

BZ filed a 6-K on March 18 unveiling its latest numbers.

The group's normalized "Operating Profit/OP" was 37% higher year-on-year to CNY 0.90B during the Oct-Dec '25 period. This represents a 7% beat against S&P Capital IQ consensus.

Also, its 4Q2025 OP-to-sales widened by 7.2ppts to 43.3% on a YoY basis. That's 2.5ppts ahead of the sell-side's 40.8% projection.

In my view, growth acceleration and leaner operations were responsible for its solid performance.

BZ's recent three-month topline of CNY 2,079M came in above the upper-end of the CNY 2.05-2.07B management guide. The implied 14.0% YoY rise was faster than the +13.2% seen for the preceding quarter. I believe that efforts to deepen its presence in specific client and geographical segments are paying off.

The firm shared at the analyst meeting that 4Q25 "revenue contribution from third-tier and low-tier cities approached 25%." The same metric was at a low-teens level in '21.

Sales generated by "enterprises with fewer than 100 employees" were up 21% YoY in Q4 according to its briefing disclosures. That's better than the 14% company-wide increase.

Separately, its total OpEx declined 7% YoY to CNY 1.36B for the final quarter of the previous year.

The "Sales & Marketing" or "S&M" costs-to-revenue for BZ went down from 4Q2024's 20% to 4Q2025's 16%. At the results call, it explained that strengthened "sales efficiency" and the absence of "major marketing campaigns" contributed to this substantial reduction.

Positive Indicators For The Year Ahead

Analysts are currently forecasting that BZ's turnover expansion will improve from +12% for FY25 to +13% in FY26. I think's that's achievable. These figures were obtained from S&P Capital IQ.

UBS Group (UBS) issued a note (inaccessible in public) titled "China Internet Sector: Online Recruitment" on March 11, 2026. The European bank polled over 200 Mainland Chinese corporate officers towards the end of last year. It found that the proportion of respondents "planning to increase spending" on Kanzhun's portal increased from 3Q25's 43% to 4Q25's 54%. This metric for its other rivals fell during the same timeframe.

The amount of time users devoted to BZ's mobile application jumped 60% YoY in the first month of '26. The December 2025 growth rate was +18%. I sourced these statistics from Nomura's (NMR) Feb 24 report (not publicly available) named "Monthly App Tracker." NMR's research also indicated that competing domestic platforms like "51Job" and "Zhaopin" witnessed relatively weaker mid-teens increases in time spent for January.

You should be aware of management's favorable market observations too. BZ mentioned at the Q4 briefing that its site's "average daily job postings in the 15 days after the spring festival grew (YoY) by a double-digit percentage."

I'm encouraged by its successful venture across the border. Kanzhun is already an established leader in Mainland China as outlined above. Its earnings call remarks suggested that the sub-brand "OfferToday has now ranked #1 in Hong Kong in terms of daily active users." My opinion is that this could provide further upside to its 2026 revenues.

BZ Is Becoming More Investor-Friendly

On the same day of its results announcement, BZ published a press release disclosing a formal "shareholder return target." It means that "no less than 50% of the Company’s adjusted net income" will be paid out to stockholders for '26-28.

I estimate that the stock's forward shareholder yield is a reasonably decent 4.7%.

The sell-side has projected that BZ's net income will go up 10% to CNY 4.0B (source: S&P Capital IQ). I assume that the firm should distribute half of that as buybacks and dividends in the near future. The consensus prediction appears realistic to me, as I've identified signs implying that Kanzhun performed well in early-2026.

Its actual stockholder return might be even more significant. At the results briefing, BZ stressed that "we will scale up repurchase as appropriate in response to changing market conditions." I infer from this statement that it's willing to buy more shares if the stock price come under pressure due to a broad sell-down.

You Need To Watch These Downside Factors

Demand for job matching in China services could drop, if the Middle East's ongoing geopolitical crisis eventually leads to a global economic downturn.

BZ's capital allocation priorities might change over time. A potential move to set aside less cash for repurchases/dividends would disappoint yield-hunting investors.

The Final Word

I continue to rate this name as 'Buy'.

My late-Nov '25 piece benchmarked BZ's valuations with its Asian peer "Recruit Holdings" (RCRRF) (6098.T). I do the same here.

As per S&P Capital IQ, Kanzhun and RCRRF trade at next twelve months' P/Es of 11x and 17x, respectively.

BZ's potential upside is +55% (17/11) using its listed comparable as a reference. My take is that its shares deserve a re-rating considering the enhanced shareholder-friendliness and a 4Q25 OP beat.

data.title: Micron Is Rewriting The Memory Cycle
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I believe Micron (MU) is no longer a cyclical memory stock but rather a fundamentally mispriced AI infrastructure stock. I’ve been bullish on this memory cycle from early on, and since my last coverage, Micron has moved another 12% higher, reinforcing that conviction.

I see pricing, not volume, driving the growth, and this is being underpinned by ongoing supply chain issues and increasing memory intensity in AI systems. With gross margins accelerating towards 80% and long-duration customer contracts emerging, I believe there’s underlying durability in the stock that the market is currently discounting. So, if you believe this continues, Micron’s underlying earnings potential is much, much greater than what the current stock price suggests.

Pricing Power Is the Real Story, Not Volume

MU’s story has changed, and it is not about volume but about pricing, and I think this changes everything. If you really dig into the numbers, you’ll see that DRAM bit volumes were only up mid-single digits and NAND bit volumes were only up low single digits. So, not exactly a rocketship. But pricing? That’s a different story. DRAM pricing was up in the mid-60% range, and NAND pricing was up in the high 70% range. That’s where the revenue growth, and much more importantly, the margin growth, is happening.

And I think this matters because, fundamentally, pricing tends to behave differently than volume. Volume can disappear rapidly. Pricing, especially in the face of underlying supply chain issues, tends to persist for longer than people expect. Management has confirmed this as well. They are currently only able to satisfy 50% to 2/3 of the demand in some cases. This is not a balanced market but a supply-constrained market, and when you add in the gross margins that are moving up towards 80%, you can see that this is a supply issue, not a demand issue.

The Quiet Shift: Memory Is Becoming Contracted Infrastructure

I think one of the things that investors are not focusing on enough in this entire discussion is the introduction of the Strategic Customer Agreements, or SCAs. While this may not have gotten as much attention as the numbers, in retrospect, this might be just as important. Micron has just signed their first 5-year SCA, moving beyond the traditional 1-year LTAs. While this might not be a huge deal in and of itself, when you think about it, you realize that this is a big deal because the relationship between the supplier and the customer is changing.

What this is saying is that the customers, the hyperscalers, the AI infrastructure providers, cannot afford uncertainty anymore. And so they need these agreements in place so they know they have the memory they need as they are rolling out their AI infrastructure. The interesting part is that Micron is in a position where they have the power in these negotiations due to the supply constraints.

What is even more interesting is the management’s hinting at a closer working arrangement in terms of R&D and product roadmaps. I think this is where the business model begins to change. Not dramatically, perhaps, but gradually. Memory is still a hardware play, and it’s still a commodity and a critical part of AI infrastructure. However, it’s starting to feel just a little bit less like a commodity and a little bit more contracted around AI infrastructure. If that trend continues, well, that’s going to influence volatility and valuation in interesting ways.

HBM4 and the Bigger Shift: Memory Is Now the Bottleneck

Pricing may explain what’s happening in the present, but I think architecture explains what’s going to happen in the future. And what’s happening in AI architecture right now is that memory is front and center. Micron’s announcement around HBM4 which delivers over 2.8 TB/s bandwidth, is significant in and of itself.

As AI models get bigger, context windows get bigger, and inference workloads get bigger, suddenly we’re in a world where we need more and more memory to store and deliver that data. And that’s why the new architecture emerges that’s more memory-intensive, not in spite of memory but because of memory.

Look at AI servers; they now require significantly more memory per unit. Workstations are shipped with 128 GB memory configurations, and smartphones are rapidly migrating to 12 GB+ memory configurations, from under 20% penetration just last year. These are structural changes in memory content, not incremental changes, and what’s more, alternative architectures are not reducing memory requirements; they’re increasing memory requirements. Efficiency plays are just as memory-intensive as everything else because they require large memory pools to work at all.

So, when management talks about memory being a “defining strategic asset,” I think they’re right. That’s because, if memory is the bottleneck, then the companies controlling the bottleneck have pricing power, and this is what’s happening.

Valuation Disconnect: Pricing a Cycle, Missing a Structural Shift

I still think the stock is a strong buy because the valuation does not take into account the extent of the earnings reset currently underway. At $444/share, are only trading at 7.9x FY2026 EPS estimates and 4.8x FY2027 EPS estimates based on consensus estimates of $58 and $95.53 respectively. That is an exceptionally low valuation for a company growing revenue by 192% and 45.0% respectively in the next two years to $109.18 billion in FY2026 and $158.3 billion in FY2027.

What I find interesting is the fact that even after the strong move up, the shares are only trading at 12.6x forward P/E, around 42% lower than the sector median. Similarly, the shares are only trading at 13.41x forward GAAP P/E, 53.8% lower than the sector median. While I agree that the company is trading at an elevated price based on the EV/Sales ratio, I think the earnings are going to grow quickly, and the market is still treating the company like a memory company when the evidence is building for a much stronger story from the company's AI infrastructure segment.

The Real Risk: Supply Catches Up Faster Than Expected

I think the biggest risk is mean reversion, which means that supply grows in a faster-than-expected fashion, and/or AI demand grows in a slower-than-expected fashion. The HBM competitive landscape and how Micron competes in the higher-end segments are a risk, as is the risk of higher CapEx spending, which is now above $25 billion. There is a geopolitical risk related to export controls and the impact they have on the China demand dynamics, and frankly, the risk that the SCAs are not successful in maintaining discipline in the downturn and the market is justified in its skepticism.

However, I still don’t see supply normalizing as fast as the market is anticipating. While Micron is guiding more than $25 billion in CapEx spending, the problem isn’t the spending; it’s the time and the complexity. New fabs have long lead times, new nodes have reduced bits per wafer, and HBM has significantly higher capacity requirements per unit of output. Meanwhile, inventory levels are still tight and customers are still under-allocated, which just further confirms that supply hasn’t caught up yet.

Takeaway

I would not focus as much on the peak in the memory cycle and more on whether this is a structurally different cycle. If supply is still constrained and AI demand is still compounding, then Micron’s margins are not going to revert as fast as people might think they are. The opportunity is in understanding this before the market fully prices it in.

data.title: Rewriting The Hedge Fund Playbook: Lessons From 2008
data.description:
As the hedge fund industry hovers near an all-time high of roughly $5 trillion in assets, up from about $600 billion at the turn of the century, it has recently experienced a moderate drawdown across most strategies in the first half of March 2026. Many investors view this as a temporary setback driven by volatility linked to the Iran war. However, if this is not a short-lived disruption, and losses deepen into sustained double-digit declines, will the implications for the industry resemble those experienced during the 2008 financial crisis? During that period, widespread drawdowns triggered investor redemptions, which in turn amplified losses in less-liquid strategies and created a negative feedback loop between performance and capital flows.

The industry has evolved meaningfully since 2008, leaving it better positioned to navigate periods of market stress. The following structural changes have made a similar unravelling less likely:

Performance: Expectations vs. Reality

Prior to 2009, many hedge funds and fund-of-funds marketed their strategies as delivering absolute returns, often targeting benchmarks such as T-bills plus 400 basis points. This framing led investors to expect consistent positive performance regardless of market conditions. As a result, many were surprised when the average hedge fund declined approximately 18% in 2008, triggering significant withdrawals. Redemption risk increases when realized outcomes diverge sharply from expectations.

Since then, managers have set more realistic expectations and educated investors on how their strategies are likely to perform across different market environments. Many investors now understand that the majority of hedge fund strategies exhibit some degree of correlation to broader markets, especially during market stress, and are therefore more prepared to withstand periods of underperformance.

Due Diligence and Transparency

In the early 2000s, operational due diligence was often limited and, in some cases, largely overlooked. Investment decisions were frequently made after only one or two meetings, with a primary emphasis on performance rather than operational robustness. Transparency was also constrained, with many managers providing limited visibility into their investment processes or underlying positions, resulting in an industry heavily reliant on trust.

This approach changed dramatically following two pivotal events: the Bernie Madoff Ponzi scheme and the Lehman Brothers collapse. Together, these events exposed significant weaknesses in both due diligence and operational infrastructure. In response, investors have adopted far more rigorous and institutionalized processes, placing greater emphasis on transparency, independent verification, and risk management. As a result, manager selection has become more disciplined, and overall industry integrity has improved.

Due Diligence Reforms After the Madoff Fraud

The uncovering of the Bernie Madoff Ponzi scheme sent shockwaves throughout the hedge fund industry, triggering significant redemptions, particularly from investors in fund-of-funds with direct or indirect exposure. The episode exposed fundamental shortcomings in due diligence practices, especially among intermediaries whose primary role was manager selection and oversight.

Beyond the immediate losses, the fraud undermined broader investor confidence. Many allocators recognized that they had not conducted sufficiently rigorous due diligence and, in some cases, lacked a clear understanding of underlying exposures due to limited transparency. This uncertainty raised concerns about undiscovered risks elsewhere in the industry, prompting further redemptions and a reduction in allocations.

Prime Brokerage Custody Structures and Counterparty Risk

In the aftermath of the Lehman Brothers collapse, many hedge funds were forced to confront the complexities of their prime brokerage custody arrangements. In some instances, assets were held in the name of the prime broker as rehypothecated collateral, enabling brokers to facilitate financing, support leverage, and enable short-selling.

While effective in normal conditions, this structure introduced significant counterparty risk. When Lehman failed, certain assets were treated as part of the firm’s general estate, requiring hedge funds to stand alongside other unsecured creditors in recovery proceedings. This highlighted the importance of asset segregation and legal ownership. In response, investors have strengthened operational due diligence, with a focus on custody structures, rehypothecation terms, and the quality of service providers, improving overall investor protection.

Alignment of Liquidity Terms and Underlying Investments

In the years leading up to the 2008 financial crisis, many hedge funds offered monthly liquidity with 30-day notice periods to attract fund-of-funds capital. In doing so, managers often overlooked the mismatch between offered liquidity and the liquidity of underlying investments.

This misalignment remained manageable during periods of positive inflows but was exposed when allocations halted and redemptions surged. Many funds were forced to impose gates or suspend redemptions, which eroded investor confidence. Funds that honored liquidity terms were disproportionately burdened by withdrawals and became sources of liquidity for investors. In response, both managers and allocators now focus on aligning liquidity terms with underlying assets, with investors more willing to accept longer lock-ups for less-liquid strategies and less tolerant of structural mismatches.

Reduced Use of Leverage by Investors and Managers

Heading into 2008, many hedge fund investors (especially fund-of-funds) used leverage to enhance returns, assuming diversified portfolios would deliver stable performance. This approach proved highly vulnerable during market stress.

As losses mounted, leverage amplified declines and triggered margin calls, forcing investors to redeem capital from underlying managers. These pressures were compounded by concurrent redemptions from their own clients, creating a cascading effect. Many fund-of-funds were forced to deleverage, experienced significant outflows, or exited the market. Since then, leverage at the investor level has declined, with a greater emphasis on liquidity, transparency, and risk management.

Evolution in the Composition and Quality of the Investor Base

At the time of the 2008 financial crisis, the hedge fund investor base was dominated by fund-of-funds, high-net worth individuals, and family offices, which are groups often viewed as more reactive to short-term performance. This contributed to rapid outflows during periods of stress.

The investor base has since shifted toward more stable institutional capital. Pension funds, along with endowments, foundations, sovereign wealth funds, and OCIO platforms, now represent the majority of industry assets. These investors typically have longer time horizons and more structured decision-making processes, contributing to greater stability.

Additionally, the quality of capital has improved. Endowments and foundations have strengthened liquidity management following challenges in 2008 related to private equity allocations and capital call dynamics. Fund-of-funds have also undergone significant consolidation, with remaining players adopting more rigorous due diligence, reduced leverage, and improved investor communication. Collectively, these changes have resulted in a more resilient and better-informed investor base.

Increased Concentration and Crowding Risk

While the industry is more operationally robust than it was in the early 2000s, there has been an increasing flow of capital to a small number of the largest multi-manager platforms. Larger investors appreciate the scale of these pod shops which can manage larger ticket sizes. Additionally, by hedging out risk across their portfolio in real time, multi-manager firms can mitigate drawdowns during moderate periods of stress.

However, many of these firms hold overlapping positions in the same securities, and during a major market event, selling by one firm can generate losses for others following similar strategies. In this environment, simultaneous de-risking across the multi-manager platforms can lead to unexpectedly large losses. This risk will be put to the test during the next major market sell-off, and allocators can mitigate their exposure by allocating to less-crowded strategies and differentiated managers.

Summary

Hedge fund industry net flows will prove more resilient in a sustained drawdown than they were during the 2008 financial crisis. Rather than broad industry outflows, we would see a rotation of capital between managers as investors reposition portfolios toward strategies perceived to be more resilient while redeeming from underperformers. In this environment, industry assets may remain stable overall, but the benefits will accrue disproportionately to high-quality managers.

Original Source: Author

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: The Fed's Inability To Spur Job Growth Is More Concerning Than High Energy Prices
data.description:
A lot of people are rightly concerned about rising energy prices and inflation. 2025 saw more tame inflation than many anticipated, but the closure of the Strait of Hormuz has sent oil prices skyrocketing. This will hurt drivers at the gas pump, and could lead to higher energy bills and more expensive goods as most plastic is derived from oil. Further, crucial fertilizer inputs have been cut off by the closure, which could impact food prices.

Interest Rate Cuts Are Failing To Create Jobs

While the closure of the Strait of Hormuz shouldn’t be overlooked, arguably, the more important development this week was Federal Reserve chair Jerome Powell admitting that job growth has been nonexistent over the past year. This comes even after the Federal Reserve carried out several rate cuts in an effort to stimulate the economy and spur job growth. Unfortunately, I believe we’re starting to see a decoupling between low interest rates and job creation, and in a worst-case scenario, we may start to see the opposite: rate cuts fueling job loss.

The Federal Reserve has two prime goals: A) Keep inflation low, with a general target of 2%, and B) encourage full employment. These goals are somewhat contradictory. Full employment can encourage inflation simply because workers have more money to spend. Low inflation, meanwhile, often depends on high interest rates, and high interest rates can cool the labor market as the costs of doing business become more expensive.

Traditionally, the Federal Reserve has been able to manage inflation and employment through the use of the Federal Funds rate. In theory, rate cuts should stimulate job creation by making it cheaper for businesses to borrow and invest. Indeed, a weak labor market has been one of the reasons the Federal Reserve has cut interest rates over the past year. Yet even as the Fed moved to cut rates, hiring has been tepid at best.

How can this be? There are a few theories, and it’s likely that multiple factors contributed. For one, during the COVID-19 pandemic, many companies massively expanded their headcount. While the COVID-19 pandemic is long in the rearview mirror, the impact of over-hiring is still felt to this day. Further, DOGE cuts to the federal bureaucracy have resulted in many thousands of people entering the labor market, many of whom have extensive experience and education.

Then there’s the continual rollout of Artificial Intelligence. AI gurus and other experts are warning that AI already is costing jobs and could cost many millions more jobs in the not-so-distant future. Interestingly, low interest rates might actually encourage the adoption of Artificial Intelligence, and as such, could increase unemployment. Researchers have already found that low interest rates can encourage the adoption of automating technologies, which tend to be CapEx-heavy. This research didn’t focus on Artificial Intelligence, but AI itself can be CapEx-heavy to implement.

Low interest rates will make it easier for companies to raise the funds necessary to invest heavily in AI, which can produce high upfront costs but also long-term savings. In this scenario, the Federal Reserve’s efforts to cut rates to stimulate hiring could produce the opposite effect, causing people to be replaced by automating technologies. This would help explain why job growth has been essentially non-existent even as the Federal Reserve has cut interest rates in an effort to stimulate the economy.

Interestingly, Amazon founder Jeff Bezos has been working to raise $100 billion to then invest in industrial companies, with the aim of spurring them to use AI to increase efficiency. This might work, but would also likely create job loss, hinting at the tricky environment Fed officials have found themselves in.

Is Inflation Decoupling From Fed Tools As Well?

Besides the jobs decoupling, there’s a risk that the Federal Reserve is losing its ability to tamp down inflation with rate cuts. With inflation set to rise owing to surging energy prices due to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, some are predicting rate hikes in an effort to cool inflation. Yet I doubt that rate hikes will cool inflation as much as hoped. Certainly, rate hikes won’t unblock the Strait of Hormuz, and energy prices will remain elevated so long as the Strait is closed.

Just as crucially, economic growth and many inflationary pressures are being driven by extensive investments in Artificial Intelligence and it's unlikely that AI investing will slow down substantially anytime soon. AI has largely evolved into a “first gets most” sort of race. OpenAI (OPENAI), Anthropic (ANTHRO), and Alphabet (GOOGL) appear to have pulled ahead of everyone else, including Meta (META), xAI (X.AI), and other developers have fallen further behind, although a major update could quickly close that gap.

If an AI company were to achieve true Artificial General Intelligence (AGI), meaning an ability to match if not exceed human capabilities in most areas, it could be worth many trillions of dollars. Already, trillions of dollars are being poured into AI, and simple logic dictates that this is unfolding because the spenders believe they can secure profits meriting the risks should they succeed.

Many workers too are fully locked in, with a senior leader at Google DeepMind, Madhavi Sewak, noting:

“Everyone is working all the time. It’s extremely intense. There doesn’t seem to be any kind of natural stopping point, and everyone is really kind of getting ground down. Even the folks who are very wealthy now … all they do is work. I see no change in anyone’s lifestyle. No one’s taking a holiday. People don’t have time for their friends, for their hobbies, for … the people they love.”

Anyway, these companies are going to continue to invest in AI even if rates climb. Smaller, poorly-capitalized companies may cut spending, but for the leading frontier developers, the potential rewards are so high that many will continue to invest heavily. Higher interest rates might increase the costs of doing business, but rather than cutting back on investments, many companies will suck up the costs and when possible, pass rising costs onto customers. As such, higher interest rates might actually increase inflation.

Why I Could Be Wrong

Perhaps Artificial Intelligence is playing only a minor role in the labor market and especially in unemployment. While companies are citing AI as a reason for layoffs, it’s possible that at least some of them are simply whitewashing. Layoffs could instead be due to offshoring, bad management decisions, over-hiring, etc. AI simply looks better to shareholders compared to management mistakes, and thus, business leaders cite AI.

Could this be the case? The most likely scenario in which this would occur is if the tepid hiring exhibited by companies was instead due to tariffs and government policy uncertainty, especially in the United States. Clarity might, in the near future, spur hiring.

Key Take-Away: Investors Should Be On Their Toes

I’m not suggesting that investors get out of the market. Certainly, I’m not doing that myself. However, I do believe the risks here are pretty high. I also believe the economy is a sort of house of cards and could be prone to collapse. It’d be wise for investors to keep an eye on the economy, and especially employment trends.

data.title: Satellogic: Upgrade To Buy As Space Intelligence Demand Explodes
data.description:
Satellogic (SATL) stock has lost 16% since my last report, in which I marked the stock a speculative buy, but with a hold rating (we mark speculative opportunities as either buy or hold based on growth visibility). The stock performed rather well, gaining over 60% before a stock offering brought the share prices down again, marking the risk for companies that are in the scaling phase. In this report, I revisit the investment case for Satellogic and update my price target as I upgrade the stock to buy.

Earth Observation Provides A Big Opportunity

The opportunity to capitalize on earth observation services remains significant, with opportunities in the commercial as well as the national security markets. The war in Ukraine as well as the war that erupted in Iran are just two recent examples of the need for earth observation as part of the intelligence stack for security purposes. The evolving threat environment layers on top of the push for sovereign solutions in Europe, which does benefit U.S.-based companies such as Satellogic. The risk is that Satellogic is definitely not the only company that provides Earth observation solutions, and in Europe we may also see a push for space solutions provided by European companies. So, it is a market with significant growth opportunities but also with competitive forces.

Satellogic Is Gaining Momentum As Space-Based Intelligence Demand Increases

For the full year, the company reported 38% sales growth to $17.7 million, with 90% of the revenues coming from data & analytics and the remaining 10% coming from space systems sales, as some customers simply elect to purchase a satellite rather than leveraging the CaaS, or Constellation-as-a-Service that Satellogic offers. Nearly 70% of sales were derived from North America, with the remaining 30% primarily attributed to Europe and Asia. I believe that those markets also are where we can expect growing demand for space-based security solutions, given the active conflict zones.

The operating loss of $31 million was a 41% improvement, while the adjusted EBITDA loss improved 48% to $17.4 million. During the year, the company had an operating cash burn of $26.9 million, which was an improvement from the $35.9 million. We did see CapEx increase from $5 million to nearly $7.5 million. That is interesting given that I spoke to Satellogic’s CFO, Rick Dunn, in December 2024 and was told that the company was sized to deliver 10x the revenues that they did in 2024. In other words, they were operating at 10% of revenue capacity and would not require substantial CapEx to realize additional growth. In my view, the increase in CapEx is a measure of confidence in future growth.

Satellogic Is Looking Increasingly More Attractive

For Satellogic, I initially expected revenues of $15.35 million in 2025 with operating cash flow of -$26.9 million and CapEx of $5.3 million and a $118 million capital raise, which would also match their year-end cash balance. The company beat expectations on revenues by 11.2%, but operating cash burn was 16% higher than expected and CapEx was 40% higher than anticipated, while the capital raise was 5% smaller, leading to a cash balance that was 13.5% lower than anticipated. These look like major differences, but given the emerging phase of the company, differences like these can be expected.

In 2025, the company booked 38% sales growth, and their backlog indicates that this year’s growth could be 40%. So, we definitely do see an acceleration in growth driven by end market demand increasing. I previously mapped revenue growth at roughly 20%. I am also mapping in growth CapEx. That would require roughly $150 million in capital to be raised incrementally over the coming years and explains the company’s $200 million shelf for Class A stock. Based on these metrics, I believe that against a 24x P/S, which is in line with the company’s median P/S ratio, the stock is more or less fairly valued and provides 23% upside with a $4.04 target when using expected sales growth for 2026 and a $5.32 price target, indicating 62% growth when using the sales expected for one year ahead. In the case that CapEx is kept constant at roughly $7.5 million, it would not alter the 2026 target and expand the upside for 2027 by only two points. In the years after, the upside would be more meaningful. While I do provide an overview on upside all the way through 2035, we do note that as the company turns EBITDA positive, the P/S valuation method becomes less useful to assign price targets as the EV/EBITDA method becomes more meaningful.

In the case that long-term growth is closer to 20% annually, we note that the price target for 2027 would be closer to $4.60 indicating 40% upside. With the company being better capitalized today, I am upgrading the stock to buy with a $4.04 base case price target and a bullish-case target of $5.32. In our projections, we are potentially being somewhat conservative, as we assume that dilution happens using current share prices rather than the target prices. If we were to use target prices, the price target in the more optimistic case would increase to $5.51.

Conclusion: Satellogic Is Better Capitalized For The Future

I previously had a hold rating on Satellogic driven by the continued need for capital raises, and while the need for additional raises is still there, we are also seeing that the company is better capitalized while the demand environment is evolving in favor of parties such as Satellogic. As a result, I am marking the stock a buy.

data.title: Paramount Skydance: A Debt-Heavy, Risky, Long-Term Stock
data.description:
David Ellison came out the winner in the Game of Streaming Thrones with Netflix (NFLX) - Paramount Skydance (PSKY) now gets to combine HBO with P+.

Ellison took the entire portfolio of Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD), including the legacy cable assets, which is an interesting move in that at this time he has indicated he will not divest the struggling collection of businesses.

In this article, I will give some thoughts on the deal. I believe PSKY is a very interesting stock for the long term, but since I last wrote about the company, stating that the pursuit of WBD made me consider Ellison in a new light, one that was very Bob-Iger-like (that probably is hyperbole, but what I mean is, he's serious about the Hollywood IP game), I believe the risk has gone up and become very speculative, mostly because of the submitted capital structure according to the recent presentation and conference on the transaction.

It will take a while to close this deal, so I also want to consider the recent fourth-quarter earnings report from February 25, 2026, as well as the conference-call insights.

PSKY Minus WBD

What should we make of PSKY without the acquisition of the Warner assets? Basically, it's a media company run by a tech scion who is having fun in Hollywood living out his dream of being a mogul.

But multiple expansion and positive sentiment can come out of that, right? Sure, depending on how he communicates strategy and what the numbers say. So, let's briefly point out a few Q4 stats.

The company missed on the top and the bottom lines: EPS was off by eleven pennies at a loss of $0.12, while the top line was off by a small amount ($20 million) at $8.15 billion, so no big deal there. Cash from operations was roughly $650 million in 2025 versus $750 million in 2024; please note if you read through the report at the investor site, the company divides performance before and after the acquisition and has restated some numbers for streaming because of free-trial exclusion (with which I heartily agree). OIBDA on a non-GAAP basis was basically the same in fiscal 2024 and 2025, roughly $3.1 billion. Revenue for theatrical was up by 16% during Q4, which is a good thing considering, as we shall see, that the multiplex strategy is what the company is partially pinning its growth on. P+ ARPU was up 10%, which is the same percentage increase that the DTC revenue saw, while Pluto TV saw a decline in revenue of 16%, which management characterized, somewhat circumspectly in both the press release and the conference call, as monetization headwinds - I take this to mean that all of the content on the FAST platform didn't see proper attachment of ad loads (engagement was stated as essentially healthy, however).

The company believes it will generate $30 billion in revenue in fiscal 2026 for an expansion of 4%. Adjusted EBITDA should come in at $3.8 billion, excluding stock compensation. Free cash flow is expected to come in at about 5% of EBITDA, or $190 million; this, management hopes, is the likely bottom for free-cash performance. The future drivers of the performance were highlighted in the streaming and advertising parts of the business.

The numbers here basically show that Paramount has a lot to prove, with or without a big, flashy acquisition. But it does have a big, flashy acquisition, so let us now consider it.

What Does Warner Mean For Paramount

In a word: theatrical.

And that word could have been the word for Netflix, but alas, it wasn't meant to be. Ted Sarandos may be trying to imply that it's no big deal that his side lost, that it was merely an opportunistic purchase, nothing more than that, it wasn't about corporate existentialism for the biggest streamer in the world...except, from one point of view, it sort of was in a partial sense, because Netflix obviously (in my opinion) wants to get a multiplex strategy going, something it clearly feels is important.

Paramount Skydance, of course, already has a studio with a ton of IP at its disposal - but, and here's the key, it doesn't have DC.

To me, this is what the chase for WBD was all about, even on Netflix's end: an easy entrance into franchised superheroes.

I'm not discounting the rest of the pieces; there are some very useful assets in the mix. But think about it: It's been said previously about HBO and Netflix that the latter became the former before the former could become the latter; perhaps so. And in Netflix's case, it's difficult to believe that the streaming giant wanted HBO as an asset, considering what it already built on its own.

P-Skydance, of course, could use HBO. But even more than that, for both Paramount and Netflix, it was about either entering theatrical or expanding theatrical opportunities, and it was also about becoming more Disney-like (DIS) - Bob Iger has inspired a lot of media leaders, and Ellison is no exception.

DC is a superhero factory that has helped to check the boxes: Paramount has Nickelodeon, a broadcaster (CBS), it owns the Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles, it has animated franchises such as SpongeBob and Rugrats, it's got Star Trek - there are a lot of similarities here between Disney's business model and its own, albeit on a smaller scale of zeitgeist. (It's too bad there isn't a theme park, but there are partnerships with experiences companies.)

Besides DC, PSKY will also have access to the Conjuring and Harry Potter films. Those are big tentpoles, but the thirty or so films that Paramount says it will release each year between the P/Warner studio system also have room for lower-budget stuff, especially in the horror vein. Recall that Ellison already owns, as an example, the Smile films, as well as Paranormal Activity. Friday the 13th IP (which used to be owned by Paramount before being sold to Warner) could be given a lower-budget treatment, as well as Evil Dead. Superheroes and supernatural beasts on the silver screen usually yield successful movie models.

Also: Linear Assets (And I Have One Suggestion)

The linear assets must also be considered. Yes, that industry is dying; however, an entertainment ecosystem of theatrical/streaming/cable-channels can help to cross-promote and amortize intellectual properties, as well as incubate new talent and seed fresh ideas.

I am not against holding assets core to the overall mission of tentpole storytelling via filmed entertainment. But, I think, given all the debt (which I will mention in a moment) and dilution inherent with the acquisition, that Ellison is going to have to think differently and perhaps make a sacrifice or two. This is a tough one, I concede, but:

He needs to consider, very strongly and seriously, radically reducing exposure to sports rights.

Yes, sports brings in advertising revenue, a lot of it, and live television is more valuable than non-live, but Netflix has been praised for its conservative approach to sports, preferring to program less costly one-offs here and there as opposed to buying the rights to something like the Olympics. Properties like TBS and TNT can be used in a vertical manner to syndicate content libraries from streaming and movie catalogs, especially as that content ages out, but it also costs a lot of capital to keep sports shows on those platforms. Getting out of the sports business would, in my opinion, help Ellison navigate the complex financial situation that a $100-billion-dollar+ merger implies. Focus on theatrical and streaming, not basketball tournaments; spin off the rights and stop investing in games - and if live programming proves to have a good ROI, maybe switch over to comedy shows and pop-music concerts. Granted, this may not make carriage-fee negotiations with cable companies easy on a go-forward basis, but some sacrifice of growth on that end may be worth the smaller expense profile.

The Debt

This is the big negative aspect of the story. There is a good explanation of how Paramount, with its relatively small market capitalization, made the deal work via some magical cash input in this article.

For me, debt can be a huge problem with media stocks, especially ones in consolidation. Disney, for instance, needs to de-lever, and I have always thought the leverage on Warner's side was holding the shares back (even though, as some readers pointed out in my WBD articles, management at WBD had the debt under control for the most part in terms of schedules of payment; for me, it was mostly about the perception by Wall Street).

P-Sky's debt level will stand at $80 billion once the combination closes. That's going to represent a lot of interest payments, cash that could have gone toward content generation. That's how I always look at media debt...the opportunity cost of generating and incubating new ideas, new franchises, new intellectual properties.

There's also the fact that David Ellison's father, Oracle's (ORCL) Lawrence Ellison, has put in his own money, along with Redbird Capital funds, toward the merger. This is likewise problematic because of dilution.

I'm thinking, besides cost controls, that Ellison will have to consider some asset-divestiture events, such as selling some of the Discovery reality channels (e.g., Food Network, Travel Channel) because, to me, they don't really line up well with the core business of storytelling via filmed entertainment as other properties such as TNT or Cartoon Network do.

Further dilution may come down the road if, and I say this hopefully, the stock rises on strategy execution and improved sentiment, thus allowing for secondary offerings to reduce debt; such a discussion is a long way off, of course, but it's something to keep in mind.

Risks And Opportunities

When I look at all of this and add it up, I can't help but be intrigued by the stock's potential. The big question is, what can David Ellison do to inspire sentiment for the shares? What will he do to catalyze multiple expansion? And yes, you took notice of the nuance there - I am talking for now about multiple expansion and sentiment as opposed to strict earnings growth, since, let's face it, there's going to be a lot of accounting stuff going on related to the merger, as well as acid-reflux-induction based on leverage/dilution.

Ellison will lean into theatrical and star-driven fare, is my best guess, and I say that as a right-or-wrong situation. Will he overpay for talent? Yes. That's his style, one would presume. He didn't produce a Top Gun film by paying Tom Cruise peanuts for compensation. And he certainly had no problem outbidding Netflix for Warner Bros. (with the familial assistance that only a billionaire scion can afford, of course).

But can he keep below-the-line, and actual production, costs under control? There is more opportunity there, and I assume he recognizes that; he can also simply go low-budget for many of the thirty movies he plans on placing into theaters each year. And, of course, there is the promise (or curse, according to the guilds) of artificial intelligence...presumably, if the AI trade has any meaning and isn't simply some overvalued monstrosity of market sentiment (I hope not, as I have many AI investments), this burgeoning technology will help P-Sky reduce costs for content effects, workflow, animation, etc.

Yet, going back to Tom Cruise, that's the thing about the CEO - there's no question he relishes being ensconced in the aesthetic of glamorous atmosphere surrounding the Hollywood business model, so that would be one risk to the thesis for Paramount Skydance: capital could be misallocated in the pursuit of superior silver-screen and streaming content.

Another risk is the aforementioned debt; that probably is the major one.

And here's something many may not have thought about: Will Netflix amp up its competitive instinct against Paramount?

I bet it might (and I am long Netflix, too, and in fact hope for competitive spirits to be raised post the Warner acquisition).

I would think that Netflix will still attempt to engage a theatrical strategy. While Ted Sarandos may have stated that it didn't matter whether or not Netflix walked away with Warner or not, that Netflix would be fine either way, I don't believe him. He wanted the legendary company/trademark, and I think he still wants to be in the business of releasing movies to multiplexes. I dont' think that desire went away with a bid of $31 per share; in my opinion, it remains, it's only going to get stronger, and I imagine we will be seeing news flow about an increase in the number of Netflix productions headed to theaters - this time with lengthier windows attached. Other things could conceivably happen behind the scenes, such as bloodier battles for talent - remember, the Duffer Brothers of Stranger Things fame went over to Paramount because they wanted to be in the big-screen movie business themselves - as well as second thoughts on how much content will be licensed from the competition. That's speculation, of course, so we will have to wait and see on it.

Competition can be good, though, and I see Paramount's theatrical strategy getting aggressive; I like that management wants to increase movie output and make more bets. That will yield new franchises, which I definitely put on the opportunity side. (And it's also a risk, because one or two expensive misfires can derail a film slate pretty quickly and produce sour sentiment from traders.)

Valuation/Conclusion

It is difficult to state a valuation for Paramount Skydance. Going by valuation metrics from SA, at the time of this writing, forward adjusted P/E is safely under 1 at roughly 0.5. P/sales forward is slightly under 0.4. EV/EBIT forward is under 12. Comparing this to the sector median is favorable, with Paramount coming out cheaper. Cash flow, though, either on a TTM or forward basis, isn't great, and neither would I expect it to be at this point, as the company has a lot of work ahead of it. At the time of this writing, the overall valuation is a C for the stock, which isn't too bad relatively speaking as the next phase of the company commences.

The bet here is that the acquisition causes synergies, and the increased content output, especially in theaters, brings in cash. Problem is, there will be box-office failures, and most likely limited profit potential on some films as co-financing deals are inevitably used to spread risk and talent contracts take a lot of the cash.

In many ways, a bet on this stock is a bet on David Ellison and the Ellison family fortune itself. It is a bet on increasing sentiment and better EPS performance. It is a very speculative situation, so caution is warranted. But I always am intrigued by new starts and inflection points, and the purchase of Warner Bros. Discovery could indeed be representative of positive change.

data.title: Glencore - Attractive Mining/Metals At The Right Price (But Not Now)
data.description:
When Glencore (GLNCY) and Rio Tinto (RIO) first started talking about merging their operations, I had long and thorough plans to initiate coverage on both tickers and cover the merger in detail. However, once I'd gotten some of my valuation models off the ground, the news came out that the companies abandoned merger talks about a month back. What it seems is that the merger talks couldn't get past the valuation stage and leadership (not surprising, given the history and the structure/fundamentals of both companies). This hasn't exactly been the first time this has been attempted, and Glencore PLC has made it clear that it's not the last time that it may (potentially) want this.

Scale and efficiency in this business is obviously very important. The merger would mark incredible scaling opportunities for both businesses. It would be one of, if not potentially the largest merger in mining history, with valuations talked about at about $260-$300B+. It is to be noted here that while Glencore has been making comments, it's actually Rio Tinto that is by far the larger business of the two.

As a result of the failed merger, Glencore fell by over 5% on the open market. As a value-oriented investor that likes buying quality assets cheaply, this renewed my interest in both Glencore and Rio, and I decided that despite the failed merger, I would initiate coverage on Glencore. I've been expanding my metal coverage (mining also) the last few months, reflecting not only an expanding diversification of my coverage in terms of geography (you will note more LATAM companies and Brazilian ones), but also opening up sectors (more mining overall).

It's important that investors understand that these are incredibly cyclical end markets. These end markets make or break the short-term and even medium-term realities of these companies' valuations. In order to invest in them, you need to understand that you may be in the negative or flat returns for years. This does not mean you did anything wrong, or the company did anything wrong. It simply means that the company's end markets are, at the time, prohibitive.

But as I hope that I have showcased in many of my articles, it's only a question of time before quality companies with underlying fundamentals revert to fair or above-fair value. The danger instead I see people doing is investing in companies that are neither safe nor have good fundamentals, but act as though they do, and argue for potential TAM, PEG factors or forward growth factors as though these equate to historically sound or currently sound results.

They do not.

So with that in mind, here's what I think of Glencore PLC.

Glencore PLC - A mining giant with great upside at a good valuation

In mining/metals, there are two sides to the business. You have on the one hand, the mining business, and then you have the global commodities business. Glencore does both. It not only successfully at an industrial scale, mines key metals of copper, cobalt, and zinc, it also markets these products, which means it captures efficiencies and margins usually lost between different players in the mining/metals supply chain. It also provides the company and its employees with a massive amount of expertise, and its tradition comes with a deep understanding of the market.

This alone, if coupled with a fundamentally sound balance sheet, is enough to pique my interest as a value-oriented investor.

What in turn differs Glencore from more "pure" miners, is that Glencore also operates its assets with a marketing business. It therefore has the ability to monetize its own production. As self-evident as this business model may sound to you, dear reader, it's not usually the case.

Why?

Capital management reasons. Doing both requires mixing capital-intensive asset management business with low-margin trading. Usually, the two are considered very difficult to combine. Miners tend to avoid it because of its risk/reward and complexity. Glencore PLC has grown with this model, and has both taken its "hits" for doing it (like investigations into bribery/corruption). As a comparison, Rio is a pure-play miner - it can focus solely on capital allocation, management, and ESG. It's a simplification. As it is with Glencore, it's one of the company's strengths.

Fundamentals, meanwhile, are a mixed bag. On one hand, it's safe with solid assets and good profit - on another level, the recent results have been impacted by rising debt (though from a very low level) and net profit losses. Glencore has some exposure to coal still, and this has been going negatively, with a 1H25 trading drop of over 75%. Furthermore, the company has been forced to take large write-offs. 2024 saw a big one exceeding $10B in total combined receivables and impairment expenses related in part to coal, in part to asset valuations.

I would relate the company's recent cyclicality to completely "natural" shifts for a miner and marketer like this. So, key point - understand what you are buying.

Overall, I believe Glencore PLC is in for a very good 2026. This is due to strong pricing for the company's key products, which are copper, cobalt, and nickel, as well as one of the key moves for Glencore over the past few years - a move away from the weakening coal sector. To clarify, we're talking moving away from thermal coal, while the company will still maintain its focus on coal used for the making of steel, which is likely to continue being priced highly.

This upside is already, I believe, somewhat confirmed in 2026E with a strong share price recovery from 2025 levels. The company is combining operational efficiencies and savings with strong cash generation efforts.

It's worth mentioning that, unlike many US-based miners and commodity players, the notion of a dividend tradition isn't of central importance to Glencore. The company, to confirm this, suspended its distributions during the pandemic but has since returned to them. It should be evident from my recent articles and coverage that while I find dividends to be of central importance, it's not a disqualifier if the company either doesn't have them, or chooses not to do them. It's analyzed on a case-by-case basis.

Glencore's native trading ticker is GLEN, a London-based ticker. The company carries a BBB+ rating and a current native yield of close to 2.5%. The way that the company is viewed by analysts today is a significant ramp-up of earnings in 2026E, with a stabilization of the current level for both 2027 and 2028E, with slower growth of single digits (albeit high-level single digits).

The problem or challenge given by analysts following Glencore in its current position of £5-£6/share is that the valuation, even after the drop following the no-merger news, is far too high for what the company carries in terms of risk from its business model and from the volatile end markets. This risk is best showcased in its lack of historical forecast accuracy.

As you can see here, the likelihood of company performance and valuation (at certain levels) needs to be understood in the context of this volatility. Anyone arguing that Glencore PLC is going in the direction X, needs to also qualify the risk for -X. I argue that Glencore generally is reversing in 2026E - but there is quite a bit of risk "baked into" this, obviously. It can be argued that the company's share price has not at all normalized from its merger talk assumptions. Consider for a moment that less than 1.5 years ago, it was trading at less than half of what it is today. Furthermore, the at this point relatively tired argument (to me, not necessarily the market) of "AI and electrification" growth is one I consider significantly overplayed.

I do believe that the company's assets are undervalued by the market on a historical basis, at very least the copper assets - but this obviously doesn't mean the company should trade at "any" valuation.

Those looking at Glencore as an investment should understand that the company's capital allocation for 2026-2028E will be primarily aimed at internal growth. A significant spike in the dividend payment is therefore an unlikely event, in my view. Consider also that volume guidance for 2026E is actually, across most parts, lower or on par with the previous year, especially Zinc. It's therefore fair to say that the company's upside will be dictated by pricing more than by volume, as well as savings. Thankfully, the company is estimating significant reductions in unit costs over the next few years, which should work to eliminate or at least lower volume pressure.

Moving to valuation

Valuation - While a great company, I unfortunately view it as overvalued

It should be clear from my wording that I view this company as attractive from a conservative perspective. At the right price, I'd invest. Unfortunately, the current valuation and multiples dictate that this is not a suitable time for investment.

The company's volatility and earnings metrics suggest a mix between P/E and EBITDA multiples, while also making individual adjustments where necessary for NAV valuation for individual assets. The current company average P/E is close to 30x. This is impacted by poor results between 2023-2025 - but even working from a 2027-2028E valuation, with upsides considered, the current multiple is close to 20x. This is higher than Rio Tinto is today. Of particular worry to me on an operational side is the 2024 results - poor despite a favorable market. DCF analysis suggests, based on conservative risk premiums, that the stock is anywhere from 20-30% overvalued above £5/share. I don't assume this based on the common argument that the trading division is a net risk rather than a benefit, but production issues, asset quality, and valuation (the copper production for Glencore peaked in 2014 and has mostly declined since), and a residual reliance on legacy thermal coal (which admittedly is lowering).

Counter-arguments is the rising copper potential the company is currently in. However, the rising reliance on Copper is in itself a risk I want to price for, given the common "energy transition" argument, where the visibility is actually worse than investors believe (in my view). Suggestions that there is a massive deficit incoming are countered by pricing for commodities triggering substitutions (already happening - aluminum is being used), recycling (which several of my covered companies do), and the hollow nature of the China transition argument. I believe the increase in demand from China will not be able to meet the decline from China due to its property market declines. That's beside the point that I believe the Green/AI demand is overstated.

So I take a somewhat more balanced and conservative approach to valuation.

Any used valuation metric is going to be incredibly volatile. Glencore's EBITDA multiple has a 5-year range of 3.3x to over 10x. The P/E goes from less than 11x to more than 30x. There is, unfortunately, no safe or remotely accurate way to measure or target this company. Best practice is all I can offer you. A range. I will say that market valuation targets for Glencore are atypically high, with a consensus on a premium, with a backdrop of 19 analysts that for the most part, consider the company to be a "BUY".

However, a reason or justification why we should pay a NAV premium 30%+ above that seen in 2024A, beyond the usual argument that its assets are undervalued, is not one I have found. It's also interesting that despite a gold in its mix, the company's NAV is now substantially above industry averages. Majors trade at around 0.7-0.9x typically - gold at below 0.8x, with the current valuation of 1.24x implying extremely high-quality assets. I believe this is too optimistic given the closure of assets (Mount Isa, Lady Loretta, Koniambo, Rhovan Vanadium, Mokala, Bracemac-Mcleod). The mentioned ones are all assets that are nearing LoM (life of mine) and have closed.

I therefore view this as too optimistic. Starting off with NAV, I would put a fair value at £4/share, which is about 1x (and that is still high). In terms of EBITDA, I would move to the upper average, at 6.5x. Based on a 2025E EBITDA of $13.5B and a 6.5x multiple, net of debt, we get an EV of $76.55B. With the SO of 11.29B, that comes to about £6.4/share. Similar for P/E. If I average the company to the rest of the industry, and use a conservative 2028E forecast for earnings, the implied P/E at 15-17x comes to between £ 4.5- £4.5-£6/share. I therefore believe this to be a suitable range for the business, with £6/share marking the upper range.

Averaging this, I give the company a fair value of £5.3/share.

However, that means that in order to get a 15% annualized RoR, and that's assuming you're reinvesting the dividends, you'll need to buy at below £4/share, near £3.9/share native, to be exact.

The PT is therefore £3.9/share.

Thesis

Glencore is a very solid mining/commodities marketing company with a lot of upside at the right price. Despite regulatory and operational risks, I very much like what I see and would like to buy the company at a good price. Recent price surges, however, have made the company somewhat uninteresting. I believe the company is trading at too high multiple. Based on the company's estimate accuracy, fundamentals, and yield, I would put a fair value target of £5.3/share, which in turn comes to a £3.9/share price target to clear my hurdle of 15% annualized with dividends.

I would, based on the current company share price and estimates, give the company a "HOLD" rating and wait until we see a better valuation.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (in italics).

This company is overall qualitative.This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.This company pays a well-covered dividend.This company is currently cheap.This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a price target of £3.9/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's not attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of March 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
